questions.question: What is the relationship between an **App Service plan** and an **App Service** (web app)?
options: The plan is the compute (VM fleet, OS, region, SKU) you pay for; the app is the workload that runs on it, and many apps can share one plan
The plan is the application code and the app is the deployment pipeline that ships it
Each app must have its own dedicated plan; plans cannot be shared between apps
The app is the compute tier and the plan is a logical grouping of unrelated resources for billing only
questions.explanation: The plan is the compute you pay for (VM fleet, OS, region, SKU/tier); the app is the workload running on it. Many apps and slots can share one plan and therefore share its CPU/RAM.

questions.question: What is the smallest (cheapest) tier that supports **deployment slots**?
options: Free
Basic
Standard
Premium v3
questions.explanation: Slots start at Standard (5 slots; Premium v3 gives 20). Free and Basic have no deployment slots — a classic interview trap.

questions.question: App settings configured in the Azure portal/CLI are injected as environment variables and **override** the values baked into `appsettings.json`.
questions.explanation: App Service app settings are injected as env vars, and ASP.NET Core's default builder layers the environment-variable provider after the JSON files, so the app setting with the same key wins.

questions.question: To set the nested configuration key `ConnectionStrings:Db` via an App Service app setting (env var), what name do you use?
options: `ConnectionStrings.Db`
`ConnectionStrings__Db`
`ConnectionStrings-Db`
`ConnectionStrings:Db`
questions.explanation: Nested keys use `__` (double underscore) as the hierarchy separator in env-var form because `:` is not portable across all platforms. `__` is the cross-platform standard.

questions.question: Moving a plan from **S1 → P2v3** is an example of which operation?
options: Scale out (more instances)
Scale up (bigger VM / change tier-SKU)
Autoscale
A deployment slot swap
questions.explanation: Changing the tier/SKU to a bigger VM is scale up (vertical). Scale out is changing the instance count (more identical VMs).

questions.question: A team deploys to a `staging` slot, validates, then swaps into production. After the swap, **production starts hitting the staging database**. What is the root cause?
options: Run From Package made `wwwroot` read-only and corrupted the connection string
The staging connection string was a normal app setting, so it swapped into production
ARR Affinity pinned production traffic to the staging instances
Always On was disabled, so the warm-up path never validated the database
questions.explanation: Normal app settings and connection strings travel with the slot during a swap. The staging connection string should have been marked a slot (sticky) setting so it stays pinned and does not follow the swap into production.

questions.question: Which statements about **Run From Package** (`WEBSITE_RUN_FROM_PACKAGE=1`) are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: It makes `wwwroot` read-only at runtime
It makes the deploy atomic — the app runs either the old package or the new one, never a mix
It eliminates the classic 'file in use / locked DLL' deploy failures
It requires FTPS as the only supported upload method
It lets the app write freely to `wwwroot` for runtime caching
questions.explanation: Run From Package mounts the app read-only, making the deploy atomic and avoiding locked-DLL failures. Options 4 and 5 are wrong: it does not require FTPS, and `wwwroot` becomes read-only (no free writes).

questions.question: Why does a slot swap achieve zero downtime instead of a cold start hitting live traffic?
options: The platform rebuilds the app from source on the production instances before routing traffic
The platform warms up the target slot until it returns healthy, then flips the routing (a routing change, not a redeploy)
It drains all in-flight requests and pauses the app for a few seconds during the cutover
It scales the plan out to double the instances so half can restart at a time
questions.explanation: A swap is a routing change, not a redeploy: the platform warms the target slot (restart + warm-up pings) until healthy, then flips the virtual IP routing. The previously-warm staging instances become production.

questions.question: An app on S1 stores user session **in memory**. After enabling autoscale (2–6 instances), users report random logouts and lost cart data. What is the fix?
options: Scale up to a bigger SKU so a single instance can hold all sessions
Externalize session/cache to Redis, share the Data Protection keyring, and disable ARR affinity
Enable ARR Affinity permanently so every user is pinned to one instance forever
Turn off autoscale and rely on a single instance to keep state consistent
questions.explanation: Scale-out spreads requests across instances; in-memory session is not shared. The correct fix is to externalize state (Redis), share the Data Protection keys across instances, and disable ARR affinity for even distribution. ARR affinity is a crutch, not the proper fix.

questions.question: When you configure a **SQL Server** connection string in the App Service Connection Strings section (type SQLServer/SQLAzure), what environment-variable prefix is applied?
options: `CUSTOMCONNSTR_<name>`
`MYSQLCONNSTR_<name>`
`SQLAZURECONNSTR_<name>`
`POSTGRESQLCONNSTR_<name>`
questions.explanation: Connection strings are injected with a per-provider prefix. SQL Server / SQL Azure uses `SQLAZURECONNSTR_` (and `SQLCONNSTR_`). `CUSTOMCONNSTR_`, `MYSQLCONNSTR_`, and `POSTGRESQLCONNSTR_` are for Custom, MySQL, and PostgreSQL respectively.

questions.question: Your App Service must call a **private SQL database** inside a VNet (no public endpoint). Which feature do you use, and in which direction does it operate?
options: Private Endpoint — inbound
VNet integration — outbound
Access restrictions — inbound
Private Endpoint — outbound
questions.explanation: VNet integration is the outbound feature: it lets the app reach private resources inside a VNet. Private Endpoint is the inbound feature (gives the app a private IP so clients reach it privately) — wrong direction for this scenario.

questions.question: Without **Always On**, App Service unloads the app after roughly 20 minutes of no requests, and in-process background tasks/WebJobs stop while the app is idle.
questions.explanation: Without Always On the app unloads after ~20 min idle, causing a cold start on the next request and stopping in-process background work. Always On (Basic and up; unavailable on Free/Shared) keeps it warm.

questions.question: `appsettings.json` ships with the build below, and an App Service **app setting** `Feature__NewCheckout=true` is also configured. At runtime, what does `IConfiguration["Feature:NewCheckout"]` return in a default ASP.NET Core app?
options: `false` — the JSON file is loaded last and wins
`true` — the app setting (env var) is layered after the JSON files and overrides it
It throws because two providers define the same key
`null` — `__` does not map to the `:` separator
questions.explanation: The environment-variable provider (which App Service app settings populate) is layered after the JSON files in the default builder, so the later provider wins and the value is `true`. `__` maps to the `:` hierarchy separator.

questions.question: What does the App Service **health check** path do when an instance repeatedly fails it?
options: It restarts the entire plan, taking all apps offline until the instance recovers
It removes the instance from the load-balancer rotation and, if it stays unhealthy, replaces it
It triggers an automatic slot swap to roll back the last deploy
It scales the plan up to a larger SKU to give the failing instance more resources
questions.explanation: An instance failing the health check is removed from the load-balancer rotation, and if it stays unhealthy it gets replaced — giving self-healing across the instance pool. It does not swap slots or change the SKU.

questions.question: Which of the following **never swap** with a slot, regardless of whether you mark them as slot settings? (Select all that apply.)
options: Custom domain names
TLS/SSL bindings
A normal (unmarked) app setting holding a feature flag
Scale settings (instance count / autoscale)
IP restrictions
questions.explanation: Custom domains, TLS/SSL bindings, scale settings, Always On, IP restrictions, publishing endpoints, and the slot hostname are always sticky and never swap. A normal unmarked app setting does swap with the slot by default.

questions.question: An autoscale rule scales out when CPU > 65% and scales in when CPU < 60%, with no cool-down. The instance count oscillates rapidly. What is the best fix?
options: Lower both thresholds so the rules trigger less often
Widen the gap between the scale-out and scale-in thresholds (hysteresis) and add a cool-down period
Switch the trigger from CPU to memory, which never flaps
Remove the maximum instance bound so it can settle at a stable count
questions.explanation: Flapping (rapid out/in oscillation) is prevented with hysteresis — a wide gap between scale-out and scale-in thresholds (e.g. out >70%, in <30%) — plus a cool-down between actions. Narrow, close thresholds with no cool-down cause the oscillation.

questions.subtopic: Run From Package vs zip locked DLLs
questions.question: A plain **zip deploy** to production intermittently fails with 'file in use / locked DLL' errors. Why does **Run From Package** sidestep this entirely?
options: It deploys each file individually so the runtime never holds more than one open at a time
It runs the app read-only directly from the mounted package, so `wwwroot` is never overwritten file-by-file while the runtime holds files open
It disables IIS and runs the app on a separate background worker that ignores file locks
It compresses the DLLs so they are too small to be locked by the runtime
questions.explanation: Locked-DLL failures occur because the runtime/IIS holds files open while a zip deploy tries to overwrite them in place. Run From Package mounts the package read-only and the app runs from it, so there is no in-place file overwrite to collide with open handles.

questions.question: Slot swaps are slow and occasionally serve errors during the cutover. Which combination most directly produces a warm, gated cutover?
options: `WEBSITE_WARMUP_PATH` alone, because it is pinged on every restart including the swap
Always On plus `WEBSITE_SWAP_WARMUP_PING_PATH` (with `WEBSITE_SWAP_WARMUP_PING_STATUSES`) pointing at a real readiness endpoint
ARR Affinity plus a larger SKU so the cutover has spare capacity
Run From Package plus disabling the health check so the swap is not blocked
questions.explanation: Always On keeps the target loaded/warm, and `WEBSITE_SWAP_WARMUP_PING_PATH` (with expected statuses) makes the platform only flip routing once the target slot returns healthy. `WEBSITE_WARMUP_PATH` fires on every restart but does not gate the swap itself.
